meta.description: Comprehensive behavior library for The Covert Narcissist Project. All entries follow a consistent schema mapping clinically used behaviors to nervous system activation states, regulation techniques, and evidence-informed depth layers.

evidence_tiers.tier_1: Peer-reviewed, formally operationalized, empirically studied with validated measures.
evidence_tiers.tier_2: Clinically used and practitioner-supported. Documented in clinical literature. Formal empirical research is emerging.
evidence_tiers.tier_3: Widely recognized in survivor and clinical communities. Peer-reviewed operationalization is limited but clinical plausibility is established.

nervous_system_states.fight: Sympathetic activation — hot, reactive, anger-forward. Body is mobilized for confrontation.
nervous_system_states.flight: Sympathetic activation — anxious, scattered, urgent. Body is mobilized to escape.
nervous_system_states.freeze: Dorsal vagal — foggy, dissociated, wordless. Nervous system goes quiet and internal.
nervous_system_states.collapse: Dorsal vagal shutdown — flat, depleted, hopeless. Chronic exposure pattern.
nervous_system_states.fawn: Hybrid autonomic — automatic compliance, shrinking, self-erasure as survival strategy.

regulation_techniques.box_breathing: Four counts in, four hold, four out, four hold. Activates parasympathetic response. Evidence base: peer-reviewed research on slow-paced breathing and heart rate variability.
regulation_techniques.4_7_8_breathing: Four counts in, seven hold, eight out. Extended exhale activates vagal brake. Evidence base: respiratory physiology and HRV research.
regulation_techniques.5_4_3_2_1_grounding: Name five things you see, four you hear, three you can touch, two you smell, one you taste. Sensory reorientation to present moment. Evidence base: widely used in trauma-informed care and DBT.
regulation_techniques.body_scan: Gentle attention moved through the body from feet upward. No demand, just presence. Evidence base: MBCT-derived, supported in mindfulness and trauma research.
regulation_techniques.grounding_affirmation: Brief spoken or internal statement anchoring identity and present reality. Used after fawn response to rebuild felt sense of self. Evidence base: rooted in CBT self-talk and schema therapy identity work.

categories.label: Grandiosity and Entitlement
categories.clinical_description: Behaviors rooted in an inflated sense of self-importance, expectation of special treatment, and belief in personal superiority. Grounded in DSM-5-TR NPD criteria: grandiose sense of self-importance, preoccupation with fantasies of success and power, belief in being special and unique, and requiring excessive admiration.

categories.label: Narrative Manipulation
categories.clinical_description: Behaviors that distort, revise, or control the victim's perception of reality and shared history. Includes gaslighting and related tactics that undermine epistemic autonomy. Grounded in peer-reviewed gaslighting research (Sweet, 2019; Klein, Wood & Bartz, 2025) and coercive control literature.

categories.label: Attention Extraction
categories.clinical_description: Behaviors designed to draw attention, admiration, and narcissistic supply toward the person with NPD traits. Rooted in DSM-5-TR criteria of requiring excessive admiration and attention-seeking. Grounded in network analysis research identifying need for admiration as a central node in NPD symptom clusters.

categories.label: Covert Aggression
categories.clinical_description: Indirect, deniable expressions of hostility, contempt, and dominance. Distinguished from overt aggression by plausible deniability. Rooted in clinical literature on passive-aggressive behavior, relational aggression, and covert narcissism (vulnerable subtype).

categories.label: Boundary Violation
categories.clinical_description: Behaviors that test, erode, or actively transgress interpersonal limits. Rooted in coercive control research, attachment theory, and family systems literature on enmeshment and autonomy suppression.

categories.label: Identity Erosion
categories.clinical_description: Sustained behaviors that undermine the victim's sense of self, competence, and independent reality. Related to ego erosion and identity diffusion documented in narcissistic abuse literature. Grounded in schema therapy (Young), object relations theory, and traumatic narcissism research (Shaw, 2021).

categories.label: Relational Weaponization
categories.clinical_description: Behaviors that use relationships, loyalty, and social networks as instruments of control, punishment, or supply extraction. Includes triangulation, flying monkeys, and isolation tactics. Grounded in coercive control research and IPV literature.

categories.label: Shame Induction
categories.clinical_description: Behaviors that deliberately produce shame, humiliation, or worthlessness in the victim. Rooted in psychodynamic shame research (Nathanson, 1992), attachment-based humiliation studies, and narcissistic abuse cycle literature documenting devaluation as a core phase.

behaviors.plain_language_label: Exaggerating achievements or importance
behaviors.plain_language_description: The person consistently overstates their accomplishments, talents, or status — often in ways that feel impossible to challenge without appearing envious or small.
behaviors.example: They describe a minor workplace contribution as solely responsible for a major outcome. When gently corrected, they become defensive or dismiss the correction entirely.
behaviors.internal_reframe: Their need to expand their story does not shrink yours. What you know is accurate.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: This behavior maps directly to one of the core signs of narcissistic personality — an inflated sense of self-importance that requires exaggerating achievements to feel real. In schema therapy, this is called the Self-Aggrandizer mode: a way of coping with deep feelings of inadequacy by performing superiority.
depth_layer.function: Expanding the story maintains fragile self-esteem. For the person doing it, this is often automatic rather than calculated — they genuinely experience their version as true. The cost falls on everyone around them who is expected to affirm the distortion.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from genuine confidence or cultural pride in accomplishment. The pattern to look for is rigidity — the story cannot be gently corrected without defensiveness — and the interpersonal cost to anyone who doesn't go along with it.

behaviors.response_script: You do not need to correct the record publicly. Internally hold what you know to be true. If pressed, a neutral 'I remember it a little differently' is enough. You are not required to argue.

behaviors.plain_language_label: Expecting special treatment without reason
behaviors.plain_language_description: The person expects automatic compliance with their preferences, rules, or needs — and responds with irritation, contempt, or punishment when those expectations are not met.
behaviors.example: They become visibly angry when asked to wait in line, follow a rule that applies to everyone, or when their preference is not immediately accommodated.
behaviors.internal_reframe: Their expectation is not a measure of your obligation. The rules that apply to everyone apply to them too.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: Expecting special treatment without reason is one of the formally recognized signs of narcissistic personality — the belief that the rules applying to everyone else simply do not apply to you. In family systems thinking, this behavior reflects an inability to tolerate being treated as an equal rather than as the central figure.
depth_layer.function: Anger in response to ordinary treatment is a shame defense. Being asked to wait in line like everyone else registers as an attack on status. The anger is not really about the situation — it is about the threat of feeling ordinary.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from legitimate advocacy for needs, cultural directness, or asking for specific accommodation. The pattern to look for is the automatic expectation of exception — not a specific request, but an assumption that exception is owed.

behaviors.response_script: You are not required to create an exception. A calm, brief response is enough: 'That's how it works for everyone.' Then disengage. You do not need to manage their reaction.
search_keywords: angry when they don't get their way

behaviors.plain_language_label: Using people to get what they want
behaviors.plain_language_description: The person treats others as resources rather than individuals — using relationships for personal gain or status without giving anything genuine back.
behaviors.example: They reach out primarily when they need something. Favors are accepted without real acknowledgment. The relationship feels one-sided in a way that is hard to name but impossible to ignore.
behaviors.internal_reframe: Being useful to someone is not the same as being valued by them. You are allowed to notice the difference.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: Using relationships primarily for personal gain is one of the core interpersonal features of narcissistic personality. In object relations theory — a framework for understanding how people relate to others — this reflects an inability to experience other people as fully human. Others are experienced as useful or not useful, rather than as people with their own inner lives.
depth_layer.function: Instrumental relating removes the emotional cost of genuine connection. This is not always deliberate — for some people, this is simply how relationships have always worked. The impact on the person being used is real regardless of intent.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from transactional friendship norms, reciprocal exchanges, or periods of self-focus during personal crisis. The pattern to look for is consistency across time and relationships — contact that reliably appears when something is needed and disappears when it is not.

behaviors.response_script: You are allowed to notice when contact only happens when you are needed. You do not have to be available on demand. 'I'm not able to right now' is a complete sentence.
search_keywords: only contacts me when they need something
only cares about what I can do for them

behaviors.plain_language_label: Constantly seeking praise or validation
behaviors.plain_language_description: The person consistently steers interactions toward receiving compliments, acknowledgment, or reassurance about their worth, appearance, achievements, or decisions.
behaviors.example: Every conversation loops back to something they did well. When you respond neutrally, they keep pushing until you say something more enthusiastic. Silence or mild feedback is treated as criticism.
behaviors.internal_reframe: Their need for constant affirmation is not your responsibility to fill. You are allowed to respond neutrally.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: The need for excessive admiration is one of the formally recognized signs of narcissistic personality. Research identifies it as the most central feature — the one most connected to everything else. Psychologist Heinz Kohut understood this as a developmental wound: the person never built a stable internal sense of their own worth, so they need constant external confirmation to feel okay.
depth_layer.function: Without consistent admiration, the person experiences what feels like a destabilizing collapse of their sense of self. This makes the need feel urgent and non-negotiable — not a preference, but a survival requirement.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from a healthy desire for positive feedback, needing reassurance during difficult periods, or enjoying recognition. The pattern to look for is frequency, pressure, and the way neutral responses are experienced as criticism.

behaviors.response_script: A warm, brief acknowledgment is enough. You do not need to perform enthusiasm you do not feel. 'That sounds like it went well' is a complete response.
search_keywords: wants me to tell them how great they are
needs to be told they did a good job

behaviors.plain_language_label: Talking about how special or unique they are
behaviors.plain_language_description: The person regularly communicates — directly or indirectly — that they are exceptional, misunderstood, or destined for greatness in ways that position ordinary people as inadequate by comparison.
behaviors.example: They describe themselves as 'not like other people,' reference their unique potential frequently, or imply that most people cannot understand them.
behaviors.internal_reframe: Positioning themselves above others is not an assessment of you. It is a statement about their relationship with their own adequacy.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: The belief in being special, unique, or destined for something greater than ordinary life is one of the formally recognized signs of narcissistic personality. In schema therapy, this is called the Entitlement/Grandiosity schema — a core belief that one is inherently superior and therefore subject to different rules.
depth_layer.function: The specialness narrative maintains self-cohesion. When the world does not reflect this narrative back — when the person is treated as ordinary — it feels destabilizing rather than simply disappointing.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from genuine confidence, giftedness, or spiritual belief in one's purpose. The pattern to look for is the relational function: does the statement implicitly position others as lesser? Does it require your agreement to land correctly?

behaviors.response_script: You do not need to affirm or challenge what they believe about themselves. A neutral response or a brief redirect is enough. Their identity is not yours to manage.
search_keywords: acts like they are above everyone

behaviors.plain_language_label: Not acknowledging or caring about your feelings
behaviors.plain_language_description: The person consistently fails to notice, ask about, or respond to how you are feeling — or gives a brief acknowledgment before making it about themselves.
behaviors.example: You share something hard. They say something brief and within a sentence or two are talking about their own experience. The conversation moves on before you feel heard.
behaviors.internal_reframe: Their inability to be present with you does not mean your experience is not worth being present with. It is.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: The inability to recognize or respond to others' emotional experience is one of the formally recognized signs of narcissistic personality. Neuroimaging research has found real differences in how people with narcissistic traits process emotional information — there appears to be a structural gap in the ability to feel what others feel, even when cognitive understanding is intact.
depth_layer.function: The empathy gap protects against the cost of genuine attunement. Being fully present with someone else's pain requires temporarily setting yourself aside — which is destabilizing for someone whose sense of self is fragile. This is often not a choice.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from alexithymia — difficulty identifying emotions — ADHD-related attentional challenges, depression, or cultural norms around emotional expression. The pattern to look for is consistency across relationships and contexts, not a single incident.

behaviors.response_script: You are allowed to seek support from people who can actually be there for you. Their limitation is not a verdict on whether you deserve to be heard.
search_keywords: I share something and they redirect to themselves
my feelings don't matter to them

behaviors.plain_language_label: Accusing others of being jealous of them
behaviors.plain_language_description: The person interprets criticism, disagreement, or others' success as evidence of jealousy toward them — framing others' legitimate responses as envy-driven attacks.
behaviors.example: When a coworker receives recognition, they suggest the coworker must be jealous of their own talents. When criticized, they dismiss it as the critic being threatened by them.
behaviors.internal_reframe: Your feedback or perspective is not jealousy. Naming it as such is a way to avoid receiving it.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: Believing that others are envious of you — or experiencing genuine envy toward others — is one of the formally recognized signs of narcissistic personality. Projection is the psychological mechanism at work: feelings or traits the person cannot tolerate in themselves are attributed to others instead.
depth_layer.function: Interpreting criticism or others' success as jealousy converts potential threats to self-esteem into evidence of superiority. If others are jealous, the person must be worth envying.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from accurate perception of competitive dynamics or real social hostility. The pattern to look for is automatic, reflexive framing — all negative feedback becomes jealousy, regardless of its actual content.

behaviors.response_script: You do not need to prove you are not jealous. That framing is designed to end the conversation. You can disengage: 'I hear you see it that way.'
search_keywords: says everyone is jealous of them
says people are threatened by them
can't take feedback without making it about jealousy

behaviors.plain_language_label: Acting superior or contemptuous toward others
behaviors.plain_language_description: The person communicates disdain, dismissiveness, or superiority toward others through tone, facial expression, or explicit comment — often in ways that are socially deniable.
behaviors.example: An eye roll when someone speaks. A dismissive tone when discussing others' choices. Commenting on others' intelligence or taste in ways that position them as inferior.
behaviors.internal_reframe: Their contempt is a habit they have built to manage their own feelings — not an accurate assessment of you. You are not required to accept it.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: Arrogant, dismissive behavior is one of the formally recognized signs of narcissistic personality. Research on contempt in relationships identifies it as more damaging than criticism or anger — because it communicates disgust and superiority rather than displeasure. In narcissistic presentations, contempt is less a situational emotion and more a chronic interpersonal stance. Devaluing others preemptively means the person cannot be devalued by them.
depth_layer.function: Contempt keeps others in a diminished position. It is often preemptive — by establishing superiority first, the person protects against the possibility of feeling small.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from frustration-based criticism, cultural directness, or situational dismissiveness. The pattern to look for is the chronic quality — contempt that is present across situations, and that seems to come with pleasure or indifference rather than regret.

behaviors.response_script: You do not have to absorb contempt. A brief, steady response or silence is enough. You are not required to perform comfort with being looked down on.
search_keywords: acts like everyone is beneath them

behaviors.plain_language_label: Making you question what actually happened
behaviors.plain_language_description: The person persistently contradicts your account of events, denies things they said or did, or reframes your accurate perceptions as evidence of confusion, sensitivity, or instability — until you begin to doubt your own memory and judgment.
behaviors.example: You reference something they said last week. They deny saying it with such certainty that you begin to wonder if you misunderstood. Over time, you stop trusting your own recollections.
behaviors.internal_reframe: Your memory did not malfunction. What you experienced was real.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: Gaslighting entered clinical literature in 1969 and has since been extensively studied. Recent research understands it through the lens of how close relationships shape our sense of reality — we rely on the people closest to us to help us know what is real. Gaslighting exploits that reliance. The person you trust most tells you your memory is wrong, your perception is off, your reaction is the problem — and over time, you begin to believe them.
depth_layer.function: When someone cannot trust their own perception, they become dependent on the person doing the gaslighting to define reality. That dependency is the goal — whether or not it is conscious.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from genuine memory discrepancies, honest disagreement about events, or different interpretations of the same situation. The pattern to look for is the accumulating effect: over time, you trust yourself less. That erosion of self-trust is the signature.

behaviors.response_script: You do not need their agreement to trust what you experienced. 'I remember it differently and I'm going to hold onto that' is enough. You are not required to convince them.

behaviors.plain_language_label: Rewriting what happened to favor themselves
behaviors.plain_language_description: After an event, decision, or conflict, the person retroactively revises the story so that their role becomes more favorable — claiming they always intended a different outcome or that their actions were misunderstood.
behaviors.example: A plan fails. The next day, they describe it as something they never fully supported, or claim the failure happened because others didn't follow their vision correctly.
behaviors.internal_reframe: The revision does not change what happened. You were there. Your account is valid.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: Rewriting what happened to avoid accountability is documented across clinical literature on narcissistic abuse and coercive control. It is related to gaslighting but temporally different — rather than denying events as they happen, it revises completed ones. Psychologically, it reflects an inability to tolerate being seen as fallible, ordinary, or wrong.
depth_layer.function: Revision protects the person's self-image from the threat of failure or accountability. A story in which they are never wrong does not require being rewritten — only kept consistent.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from honest reinterpretation of events over time, natural memory malleability, or genuine perspective shifts. The pattern to look for is directional consistency — revisions that always favor the narrator.

behaviors.response_script: You do not need to argue the original version publicly. Holding your own clarity internally is enough. If needed, one calm statement: 'That's not how I remember it unfolding.'
search_keywords: saying they never agreed to that
acting like the failure wasn't their fault

behaviors.plain_language_label: Shifting blame onto you or others
behaviors.plain_language_description: When something goes wrong, the person consistently redirects responsibility away from themselves and onto others — including you — regardless of the actual chain of events.
behaviors.example: They make an error at work and explain to you afterward how it was the result of someone else's incompetence, poor communication, or external interference — never their own action.
behaviors.internal_reframe: Someone else receiving blame does not mean the blame is accurate. You are allowed to hold a different assessment.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: Shifting blame onto others is documented in gaslighting research as a specific pattern — converting critical conversations about one's own behavior into critiques of others. A formal framework called DARVO describes this: Deny the behavior, Attack the person raising it, Reverse who is the victim and who caused the harm. In psychological terms, this reflects a stable attribution style that locates all causality outside the self.
depth_layer.function: Blame deflection manages shame before it can land. Accountability would require experiencing the discomfort of having caused harm — deflecting prevents that experience entirely.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from legitimate attribution of shared responsibility, contextual self-protection, or honest disagreement about cause. The pattern to look for is directional consistency — blame that never lands on the person doing the deflecting.

behaviors.response_script: You are not required to absorb blame that does not belong to you. Silence is a valid response. If pressed: 'I see it differently.'

behaviors.plain_language_label: Accusing you of what they are actually doing
behaviors.plain_language_description: The person attributes their own feelings, motives, or behaviors to you — accusing you of lying, manipulation, selfishness, or cruelty when those descriptions more accurately describe their own conduct.
behaviors.example: They are withholding affection as punishment. They accuse you of being emotionally cold. They are controlling the finances. They describe you as financially irresponsible.
behaviors.internal_reframe: What is being described to you may be a mirror of what is being done to you. Trust what you observe.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: Projection — attributing your own feelings or behaviors to someone else — is one of the foundational defense mechanisms in psychology. In narcissistic presentations, it is especially common around traits the person finds shameful: manipulation, cruelty, selfishness. These are disowned internally and perceived in others instead. Research on gaslighting specifically documents this as a tactic — the person's own behaviors are attributed to the victim.
depth_layer.function: Projection simultaneously protects the person from uncomfortable self-recognition and places the victim on the defensive. Instead of addressing their own behavior, they are now responding to an accusation.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from accurate feedback, honest pattern recognition, or shared relational dynamics. The inversion quality is the clinical marker — the accusation describes behaviors the accuser is currently enacting.

behaviors.response_script: You do not need to accept a description of yourself that does not fit. Internally, hold what you know about your own behavior. 'That's not accurate' is enough if a response is needed.
search_keywords: accusing me of what they're doing
accusing me of lying when they're lying

behaviors.plain_language_label: Mixing truth and lies to confuse you
behaviors.plain_language_description: The person presents mostly accurate information with small but critical distortions embedded — making it nearly impossible to identify or challenge the false elements without appearing to dispute the entire account.
behaviors.example: They describe a conflict to a mutual friend using accurate details but omitting the context that would explain your response, leaving the listener with a skewed impression.
behaviors.internal_reframe: A story can be technically accurate and still be misleading. You are allowed to hold the full context.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: Embedding distortions within mostly accurate information is documented in clinical literature on coercive control and narcissistic manipulation. It exploits a basic feature of trust — we lower our guard when most of what someone says checks out. The distorted elements are harder to identify precisely because they are surrounded by truth.
depth_layer.function: Plausible deniability is the goal. The person can always point to the accurate elements as evidence of their honesty. Distortion by omission or framing leaves no fingerprints.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from incomplete information, honest memory gaps, or perspective differences. The pattern to look for is directional consistency — omissions that consistently serve the narrator's interests.

behaviors.response_script: You hold the full context. You do not need to convince everyone. Save your energy for the people in your life who have shown they can handle the whole truth.

behaviors.plain_language_label: Overwhelming you with affection to gain control
behaviors.plain_language_description: The person comes in fast and intense — overwhelming you with affection, attention, and devotion early in the relationship — in a way that creates a strong bond before you have had a real chance to know them.
behaviors.example: In the first weeks they declare you are their soulmate, talk about your future together, and are in constant contact — creating a feeling of deep connection that makes it harder to trust your instincts later when things shift.
behaviors.internal_reframe: Intensity is not the same as depth. Real connection builds over time with consistent behavior. What you experienced was real — and so is the right to reassess it.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: Love bombing has been formally studied and measured in peer-reviewed research, with findings linking it to narcissism, low self-esteem, and anxious attachment. It works by exploiting the attachment system's response to overwhelming positive attention — bonding happens before enough information has been gathered to make an informed choice. The intensity of the early phase also makes later warning signs harder to trust.
depth_layer.function: Rapid emotional dependency and trust lower the other person's critical faculties. The contrast between the love bombing phase and what follows also deepens the confusion — if they were capable of that much love, how did it disappear?
depth_layer.differential: This is different from genuine enthusiasm in new relationships or culturally expressive affection. The patterns to look for are the speed of escalation, pressure for rapid commitment, and the shift in behavior once attachment is secured.

behaviors.response_script: You are allowed to slow down. Genuine care does not require you to match intensity or commit before you are ready. 'I need more time to get to know you' is a complete and valid response.
search_keywords: can't do anything wrong in their eyes

behaviors.plain_language_label: Unpredictable hot and cold behavior that keeps you hooked
behaviors.plain_language_description: The person alternates between warmth and withdrawal, praise and criticism, closeness and distance — on an unpredictable schedule that keeps you working to recover the good version of them.
behaviors.example: Some days they are warm, connected, and affirming. Other days they are cold, dismissive, or critical — with no clear reason for the shift. You find yourself constantly monitoring their mood and adjusting to try to bring back the good version.
behaviors.internal_reframe: The good moments are real. So is the pattern. You are allowed to see both.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: Intermittent reinforcement comes from behavioral psychology — specifically the finding that unpredictable reward schedules create the strongest and most persistent behaviors. A slot machine pays out randomly, which is why people keep pulling the lever. The same mechanism operates in relationships with unpredictable warmth and coldness. Research on traumatic bonding found that this pattern creates emotional bonds that persist long after the relationship ends.
depth_layer.function: The nervous system becomes organized around seeking the positive reinforcement — the warmth, the good version of the person. This creates something that functions neurologically like an addiction to the relationship itself.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from natural mood variation, stress responses, or relationship repair after genuine conflict. The pattern to look for is the unpredictability, the absence of accountability during the cold phases, and your own growing hypervigilance to their mood.

behaviors.response_script: The warmth is not the whole truth. Neither is the coldness. The pattern across time is the truth. You are allowed to look at the full picture rather than just how things feel right now.
search_keywords: never know which version I'm getting

behaviors.plain_language_label: Making promises about the future they never intend to keep
behaviors.plain_language_description: The person makes vivid, detailed promises about future plans, commitments, or changes — using these promises to secure your compliance or continued investment — without genuine intention or follow-through.
behaviors.example: They promise to change a specific behavior, plan a significant shared experience, or commit to a relationship milestone — and the promise disappears without acknowledgment once the immediate need is met.
behaviors.internal_reframe: A promise is only as real as the behavior that follows it. You are allowed to measure by action.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: Future faking is documented across clinical literature on narcissistic abuse as a tactic used during both early relationship stages and attempts to pull someone back in after they have tried to leave. It exploits hope — specifically the hope that things will become what they once seemed to be. Promises of future security keep the attachment bond active in the present.
depth_layer.function: A promise purchases compliance and continued investment without the cost of actual change. It provides just enough hope to prevent departure.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from genuine intentions that fail due to real limitations, timing differences, or differing priorities. The pattern to look for is when promises reliably appear when the person needs something and disappear once that need is met.

behaviors.response_script: You are allowed to trust the pattern more than the promise. 'I hear what you're saying. I'll see how that unfolds' is a complete and self-protective response.
search_keywords: talks about the future but never follows through
uses promises to keep me around
I keep waiting for them to follow through

behaviors.plain_language_label: Disguising insults as jokes
behaviors.plain_language_description: The person delivers cutting, demeaning, or humiliating remarks framed as jokes — then uses your reaction as evidence of oversensitivity when you respond to the content rather than the framing.
behaviors.example: They make a comment about your appearance, intelligence, or choices in a group setting. When you express hurt, they say 'I was just joking — you're so sensitive.'
behaviors.internal_reframe: You responded to the content. The content was the message. Your response was accurate.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: Disguising insults as humor is documented in clinical literature on covert narcissism and indirect aggression. Humor provides cover — the person can deliver hostility while maintaining a socially acceptable presentation. When the target responds to the content rather than the framing, the response becomes evidence of oversensitivity. This secondary move is itself a form of gaslighting.
depth_layer.function: Contempt and superiority are delivered while the persona of a non-abusive person is maintained. The humor framing is not incidental — it is the mechanism.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from actual humor, gentle teasing in relationships where both people enjoy it, or cultural directness. The pattern to look for is consistent directionality — the jokes consistently target vulnerabilities — and the secondary dismissal of pain as oversensitivity.

behaviors.response_script: You do not have to perform laughter. A steady, calm response: 'That one landed as hurtful for me.' If they dismiss it, you have learned something about the relationship. You do not need to argue further.
search_keywords: making fun of me then saying they were joking

behaviors.plain_language_label: Punishing through silence or withdrawal
behaviors.plain_language_description: The person expresses hostility or displeasure not through direct communication but through deliberate withdrawal — of affection, communication, cooperation, or presence — as a form of covert punishment. For those with anxious attachment, this produces urgent pursuit — working harder to recover the warmth.
behaviors.example: After a disagreement they become coldly polite. You find yourself reviewing the interaction repeatedly, reaching out more than usual, apologizing for things you are not sure you did wrong — trying to restore connection and end the silence.
behaviors.internal_reframe: The urgency you feel to fix the silence is your mind and body responding to the fear of losing connection — not evidence that you did something wrong.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: Using silence and withdrawal as punishment is documented in relationship research as stonewalling — one of the strongest predictors of relationship harm. In narcissistic presentations, this withdrawal is deliberate rather than overwhelmed. For people with anxious attachment — a strong fear of abandonment — the withdrawal triggers urgent pursuit: reaching out more, apologizing for things you are not sure you did wrong, working to restore the warmth.
depth_layer.function: Punishing through silence delivers the message without the accountability of words. The deniability — 'I'm fine, nothing's wrong' — places the burden of proof on the person being punished and questions their accurate perception of what is happening.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from needing space after conflict, introversion, or depression-related withdrawal. The pattern to look for is the trigger — withdrawal that correlates specifically with not getting what the person wants — and the resolution — warmth that returns when compliance is offered.

behaviors.response_script: You are allowed to name it once: 'It feels like something shifted and I'd like to talk about it when you're ready.' Then stop reaching. Chasing the silence rewards the silence.

behaviors.plain_language_label: Going numb or shutting down in response to their silence
behaviors.plain_language_description: The person's deliberate withdrawal triggers a freeze response in you — rather than pursuing, you go quiet, disconnect from your own needs, and wait in a fog of uncertainty for the silence to end.
behaviors.example: After a disagreement they become coldly polite. You find yourself going flat — not reaching out, not processing, just waiting. You feel disconnected from what you actually need or feel. Time passes and you are not sure how.
behaviors.internal_reframe: The flatness and fog are your mind and body protecting you from the pain of the withdrawal. It is a response to something real — not a permanent state. You are still in there.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: When someone we are attached to withdraws, the nervous system can respond in two directions: anxious pursuit or shutdown. The shutdown response — going flat, foggy, disconnected — is the nervous system conserving energy when active pursuit has not worked or feels impossible. In relationships where withdrawal has been used as punishment repeatedly, this shutdown can become the automatic response.
depth_layer.function: The flatness protects against the acute pain of active pursuit. It is adaptive in the moment — and exhausting over time.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from introversion, healthy need for space, or depression. The pattern to look for is the trigger — specifically the other person's withdrawal — and the quality of disconnection from your own emotional experience.

behaviors.response_script: The fog is real and it will lift. When you can, try to locate one physical sensation — your feet on the floor, your hands in your lap. You do not have to solve anything right now. Just return to your body first.
search_keywords: can't feel anything when they go cold
can't access what I need when they pull away

behaviors.plain_language_label: Giving a compliment with a hidden insult
behaviors.plain_language_description: The person says something that sounds like a compliment but has a sting buried inside it — leaving you feeling subtly put down while making it hard to respond without seeming oversensitive.
behaviors.example: 'You look amazing — I almost didn't recognize you.' 'That was really good for someone without formal training.' 'I'm impressed you managed to do that.'
behaviors.internal_reframe: You can receive the compliment and release the qualifier. You do not have to carry the sting.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: A compliment with a sting embedded is documented in clinical literature on indirect aggression and covert narcissism. It functions as a negging tactic — ambiguous negative feedback that destabilizes self-esteem while maintaining social cover. Indirect hostility through apparent niceness is more damaging to self-esteem over time than direct insults, because the person cannot easily name, process, or respond to it.
depth_layer.function: Contempt and superiority are delivered while a socially acceptable presentation is maintained. The compliment provides the cover; the qualifier does the psychological work.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from clumsy compliments, genuine attempts to acknowledge growth, or cultural differences in directness. The pattern to look for is the emotional residue — you consistently feel subtly diminished rather than genuinely affirmed.

behaviors.response_script: Receive the compliment: 'Thank you.' Full stop. You do not need to acknowledge the sting or invite more conversation about it. Taking only the positive and moving on is a form of self-protection.
search_keywords: says something nice then ruins it
compliment that felt like a put down

behaviors.plain_language_label: Using others as a standard to make you feel inferior
behaviors.plain_language_description: The person implicitly or explicitly compares you unfavorably to others — other people, past partners, family members, or cultural standards — in ways that position you as deficient.
behaviors.example: 'My ex never had a problem with this.' 'Most people in your position would have figured this out by now.' 'Everyone else seems to manage it fine.'
behaviors.internal_reframe: A comparison is not a verdict. You are not required to accept another person's measuring stick.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: Being compared unfavorably to others as a control tactic is documented in research on social comparison and shame. Being placed in an upward comparison — someone else is better, does it better, handles it better — reliably decreases self-esteem. In schema therapy, repeated comparative diminishment activates the Defectiveness/Shame schema: the deep belief that one is fundamentally inadequate relative to others.
depth_layer.function: Keeping someone in a state of inadequacy-based striving maintains compliance. If you are never quite measuring up, you keep trying to. The person with NPD traits also maintains a position of superiority — the judge of the comparison.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from constructive feedback using examples, sharing information about others' approaches, or perspective-offering. The pattern to look for is the emotional function — comparisons that consistently produce shame rather than useful information.

behaviors.response_script: You are not in competition with a comparison. 'I'm not them' is a complete internal response. If a verbal response is needed: 'I'm doing this my way.'
search_keywords: making me feel inferior by comparison
using comparisons to make me feel bad

behaviors.plain_language_label: Testing your limits to see what they can get away with
behaviors.plain_language_description: The person makes small, gradual pushes against your limits — testing each one to see what you will accept before going further. The early tests are minor and easy to dismiss. Escalation follows when those are tolerated.
behaviors.example: They make a joke you've asked them not to make. When you don't respond firmly, they escalate to a direct version of the same behavior. When you finally respond, they express surprise at your reaction.
behaviors.internal_reframe: You noticed. The fact that you noticed is information. You do not have to wait until the incursion is large to respond.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: Gradual, incremental boundary testing is documented in clinical literature on coercive control and narcissistic behavior as a calibration process — systematically mapping what will be tolerated before pushing further. In family systems thinking, this reflects an inability to tolerate the emotional separation implied by another person having distinct limits.
depth_layer.function: Each successful small incursion expands the territory of tolerated behavior. By the time the violations are obvious, a long history of earlier, smaller ones has already been accepted.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from genuinely misunderstanding limits, cultural differences in boundary norms, or single incidents. The pattern to look for is the incremental quality — tests that escalate in response to permissiveness.

behaviors.response_script: You are allowed to respond to small violations early. You do not have to wait until things are obviously out of line to speak up. Saying something calmly and early costs much less than a limit that has been worn down over time.
search_keywords: seeing what they can get away with

behaviors.plain_language_label: Controlling your choices or decisions
behaviors.plain_language_description: The person exerts pressure — through criticism, guilt, withdrawal, or explicit demands — to control your decisions, preferences, relationships, or time, positioning your autonomy as a threat to the relationship.
behaviors.example: You make a decision about your career, social plans, or personal life. They respond with hurt, criticism, or manipulation designed to reverse your choice or require their approval for future decisions.
behaviors.internal_reframe: Your choices are yours to make. Having to get approval for your own life is not what a healthy relationship asks of you — it is a form of control.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: Controlling another person's choices and decisions is formally recognized in coercive control research as a primary form of intimate partner abuse that extends beyond physical harm. In family systems thinking, this reflects enmeshment — a relational dynamic in which one person cannot tolerate the other having independent thoughts, feelings, or decisions without experiencing it as a threat or abandonment.
depth_layer.function: Autonomy suppression keeps the other person oriented toward the controlling person's preferences rather than their own. A person who must seek approval for their own decisions is structurally dependent.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from genuine concern about shared decisions, collaborative decision-making in relationships, or cultural norms around consultation. The pattern to look for is directional pressure — toward the controlling person's preferences, regardless of what the other person needs.

behaviors.response_script: You are allowed to make this decision. 'I've thought about it and this is what I'm doing' is a complete statement. You do not need to negotiate your right to decide for yourself.
search_keywords: needs to approve everything I do
pressuring me to do what they want
guilt trips when I decide for myself
needs to be consulted on everything
my autonomy feels like a threat to them

behaviors.plain_language_label: Going through your things or monitoring you
behaviors.plain_language_description: The person accesses your private communications, belongings, accounts, or location without permission — framing this as concern, love, or a reasonable right in the relationship.
behaviors.example: They go through your phone, read your messages, or check your location without asking — and when confronted, frame it as concern for your safety or evidence of their love.
behaviors.internal_reframe: Privacy is not secrecy. You are allowed to have a private interior life and private communications. Access without consent is a violation regardless of how it is framed.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: Going through another person's private communications, belongings, or accounts without consent is formally recognized in coercive control research and increasingly in law as a surveillance tactic and a form of abuse. It establishes dominance — the message is that the controlling person has the right to access any part of your life. Framing it as love or concern is a reframing tactic that recruits your attachment instincts against your self-protective ones.
depth_layer.function: Surveillance provides information that can be used for control and sends a message about power: your private interior is not yours alone. The love or concern framing makes it harder to name as what it is.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from transparent communication about shared accounts, consensual location sharing, or agreed-upon parental oversight. The pattern to look for is the absence of consent and the framing — your discomfort with the violation becomes the problem rather than the violation itself.

behaviors.response_script: You are allowed to name this clearly: 'Going through my phone without asking is not okay with me.' You do not need to justify your right to privacy or negotiate it.

behaviors.plain_language_label: Giving gifts or help that creates debt
behaviors.plain_language_description: The person offers help, gifts, or favors in contexts or ways that make declining difficult — and subsequently uses those offerings as leverage, as evidence of your ingratitude, or as a debt to be collected.
behaviors.example: They offer to pay for something in front of others, making refusal socially awkward. Later, when you assert a boundary or disappoint them, they reference all they have done for you.
behaviors.internal_reframe: Generosity that creates debt is not generosity. Receiving something does not obligate you to compliance. One warm acknowledgment is enough.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: Help or gifts that create obligation are documented in clinical literature on manipulation and coercive control. We are socialized to feel obligated to people who give to us — this is a normal social mechanism that gets exploited here. In narcissistic presentations, giving functions as investment rather than genuine generosity. The subsequent ledger-keeping and reference to past giving is a coercive control tactic documented in abuse research.
depth_layer.function: A sense of debt suppresses autonomy and preempts boundary-setting. The public nature of many offers increases the social pressure to accept.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from genuine generosity without strings, cultural norms around gift-giving, or spontaneous care. The pattern to look for is the subsequent activation — gifts and help that reappear as leverage when the person feels insufficiently rewarded or when you try to hold a limit.

behaviors.response_script: You can receive something graciously without owing anything for it. 'Thank you' is complete. If they bring it up later as a reason you should comply: 'I appreciated that — it doesn't change this situation.'
search_keywords: using what they've done for me against me

behaviors.plain_language_label: Dismissing or downplaying your experiences
behaviors.plain_language_description: The person consistently responds to your experiences, concerns, or achievements with minimizing language — reducing their significance, reframing them as overreactions, or acknowledging them briefly before moving on.
behaviors.example: 'That's not a big deal.' 'You're overthinking it.' 'A lot of people have it way worse.' Your experience is consistently treated as less significant than it feels to you.
behaviors.internal_reframe: Your experience does not require their validation to be real. The size of your response is appropriate to the size of your experience.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: Consistent minimization of another person's experiences is documented in clinical literature as emotional invalidation — and as a form of emotional abuse when chronic. Research on dialectical behavior therapy identifies chronic invalidation as a primary contributor to emotional dysregulation: when your responses are consistently treated as too much, you either learn to suppress them entirely or amplify them in an attempt to be heard. In schema therapy, repeated minimization activates the Emotional Deprivation schema — the deep belief that your emotional needs are excessive or undeserving of response.
depth_layer.function: Minimization reduces the other person's emotional footprint in the relationship, making more space for the minimizer's own needs. It also produces a subtle sense of superiority — positioning the minimizer as the less reactive, more reasonable party.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from genuine perspective-offering, support through normalization, or cultural norms around emotional expression. The pattern to look for is consistency and direction — your experiences are never quite significant enough, in every context.

behaviors.response_script: You are allowed to hold your experience at its actual size. You do not need to shrink it to make them comfortable. Internal validation is real validation: 'This matters to me.'
search_keywords: saying it's not a big deal
making me feel like I'm overreacting

behaviors.plain_language_label: Taking credit for your work or ideas
behaviors.plain_language_description: The person presents your ideas, work, or contributions as their own — either directly or through strategic omission — diminishing your authorship in shared or public contexts.
behaviors.example: You develop an idea and share it privately. In a group setting, they present it as their own discovery without attribution. When you note the origin, they minimize or deny it.
behaviors.internal_reframe: You know where it came from. That knowledge belongs to you regardless of who the room believes. Your contribution is real.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: Presenting another person's ideas or work as your own is documented in clinical literature on narcissistic behavior as a form of supply extraction — taking the recognition that belongs to someone else. In psychological terms, this reflects an inability to experience others as having independent contributions worth acknowledging — others' excellence is experienced as available for absorption.
depth_layer.function: Taking credit extracts the recognition — the admiration, the status — while eroding the other person's sense of their own intellectual and creative authority. Over time, people stop sharing ideas in environments where this happens.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from genuinely collaborative work with shared credit, forgetfulness about sources, or cultural norms around communal knowledge. The pattern to look for is the response when you name the origin — genuine collaboration does not require you to fight for acknowledgment.

behaviors.response_script: You can name your contribution once, calmly: 'That was something I brought in — glad it landed well.' Then release it. You do not need to argue your authorship repeatedly.
search_keywords: they got credit for what I did
passing off my work as theirs

behaviors.plain_language_label: Defining who you are in ways that erase your own sense of self
behaviors.plain_language_description: The person consistently defines, labels, or characterizes you in ways that contradict your own self-understanding — gradually replacing your self-concept with their version of who you are.
behaviors.example: Over time, you stop describing yourself using your own words and begin using theirs. Your interests, opinions, and self-descriptions have quietly aligned with what they have told you about yourself.
behaviors.internal_reframe: Their definition of you is not the authoritative one. You are allowed to reclaim your own language for yourself.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: Gradually replacing another person's self-concept with your own version of them is documented in narcissistic abuse literature as identity substitution. In object relations theory, this reflects the imposition of a false self onto the victim — the authentic self with its genuine preferences, opinions, and identity is replaced with a compliant version organized around the other person's needs. In schema therapy, this activates the Subjugation schema: the deep belief that suppressing your own identity is the price of relational safety.
depth_layer.function: A victim who has internalized the controlling person's definition of themselves is more compliant, less likely to leave, and more invested in the relationship's terms.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from the natural influence close relationships have on self-concept, genuine feedback that accurately reflects something about you, or collaborative identity exploration. The pattern to look for is the replacement quality — your own self-concept gradually disappears rather than expanding.

behaviors.response_script: Notice when you describe yourself using their words rather than yours. Reclaiming your own language for yourself is a form of recovery. What would you say about yourself if they had never said it first?
search_keywords: I stopped knowing what I think
I use their words to describe myself now
I don't know who I am anymore

behaviors.plain_language_label: Making you doubt your own abilities
behaviors.plain_language_description: The person consistently questions, second-guesses, or offers unsolicited corrections to your decisions, work, or judgment — creating a steady accumulation of self-doubt about your own competence.
behaviors.example: They comment on how you cooked the meal, made the plan, or handled a situation — not in a collaborative way but in a way that positions their approach as the correct one and yours as inadequate.
behaviors.internal_reframe: You have evidence of your own competence that exists independent of their assessment. You are allowed to access it.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: Consistently undermining another person's confidence in their own judgment is documented in clinical literature as an erosion of self-efficacy — the belief in your own capacity to make decisions and handle life. Research on learned helplessness shows that repeated experiences of having your efforts corrected or criticized can lead to simply stopping trying. In schema therapy, this activates the Failure schema: the deep belief that you are inherently incapable compared to others.
depth_layer.function: Creating dependency on the undermining person as the competent authority. A person who doubts their own judgment will defer to the person doing the doubting.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from genuine constructive feedback, collaborative problem-solving, or honest difference in approaches. The pattern to look for is directional consistency — corrections that always position the other person as superior — and the emotional residue of inadequacy rather than growth.

behaviors.response_script: Your judgment has been reliable — the evidence is in your history, not in their assessment of you. You are allowed to trust it. You do not need their sign-off to move forward.
search_keywords: I don't trust my own judgment anymore

behaviors.plain_language_label: Bringing in a third person to control or destabilize you
behaviors.plain_language_description: The person introduces a third party — real or implied — into the dynamic between you and them, in order to create competition, insecurity, jealousy, or to shift the power balance in their favor.
behaviors.example: During a conflict, they reference how their friend, family member, or ex would agree with their position. Or they mention another person's admiration for them at moments when you are already feeling insecure.
behaviors.internal_reframe: The third party is being used as a tool, whether they know it or not. The dynamic is between you and the person using them — not between you and the third party.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: Bringing a third person into a two-person dynamic to create competition, insecurity, or shift the power balance is documented in family systems theory as triangulation. In narcissistic relationships, it is used deliberately to maintain control — creating competition for the narcissist's approval, destabilizing the other person's sense of security, and keeping the narcissist in the center position.
depth_layer.function: Manufactured competition maintains supply, regulates the narcissist's fear of abandonment by keeping the other person uncertain of their position, and shifts power dynamics.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from naturally sharing others' perspectives, seeking mediation, or normal co-parenting communication. The pattern to look for is the timing and function — third parties introduced at moments of conflict or insecurity, with information that consistently serves the controlling person's position.

behaviors.response_script: You are not in competition with whoever they are using to make you feel insecure. That competition was created — it is not real. You can step out of it: 'I'm not interested in comparing. I'd like to talk about what's happening between us.'
search_keywords: making me feel like I have competition
making me compete for their approval

behaviors.plain_language_label: Getting others to do their bidding or pressure you
behaviors.plain_language_description: The person recruits others — often unknowingly — to reinforce their narrative, pressure you toward compliance, deliver messages, or carry out relational punishment on their behalf.
behaviors.example: After a conflict, mutual friends begin reaching out to encourage you to apologize or see the person's perspective. Family members call to relay concerns that align suspiciously with the person's position.
behaviors.internal_reframe: The people reaching out may genuinely believe they are helping. Their perspective has been shaped by one account. You are not required to accept a verdict from people who have heard one side.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: Recruiting others to deliver messages, apply pressure, or carry out relational punishment is documented in clinical literature on coercive control as proxy abuse — extending control beyond direct contact through social networks. This works by selectively sharing a version of events that positions the controlling person as the wronged party — activating the social network's protective instincts toward the wrong person.
depth_layer.function: Extends the reach of control and pressure without requiring direct escalation. Also manages the social narrative by recruiting allies before the other person has a chance to share their account.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from genuine concern from mutual friends, legitimate family mediation attempts, or normal support-seeking after conflict. The pattern to look for is coordination — multiple people delivering the same message at the same time, and that message consistently serving the controlling person's position.

behaviors.response_script: You are not required to defend yourself to people who have heard one account. A calm, brief response: 'I appreciate your concern. This is something I need to handle directly.' Then disengage.
search_keywords: sending people to talk to me
others repeating what they told them

behaviors.plain_language_label: Spreading false or distorted information about you
behaviors.plain_language_description: The person systematically shares a distorted, exaggerated, or fabricated account of your behavior or character with others — positioning themselves as the wronged party and you as unstable, abusive, or untrustworthy.
behaviors.example: After a conflict or separation, you begin to hear from mutual contacts that the person has been sharing concerning accounts of your behavior. The accounts contain distortions or outright falsehoods that you can trace back to them.
behaviors.internal_reframe: Your character is not defined by the account of someone who benefits from making you look bad. The people who know you from their own experience are the ones who matter.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: Systematically spreading distorted or fabricated accounts of another person's behavior or character is documented in coercive control research as reputational destruction — a form of abuse that isolates the victim by managing their social network. DARVO describes the structural dynamic: the controlling person positions themselves as the victim and the actual victim as the aggressor, making it socially difficult for the truth to be heard.
depth_layer.function: Pre-emptively discredits anything the victim might say, isolates them by managing the social narrative, and positions the controlling person as sympathetic.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from honest sharing of experiences after a relationship ends, accurate accounts that happen to be damaging, or normal venting after conflict. The pattern to look for is the systematic quality — intentional, coordinated, across multiple contacts — and the presence of distortion or fabrication.

behaviors.response_script: You do not need to launch a counter-campaign. Living clearly and consistently is your strongest response. For people whose opinion matters, quiet and honest conversations are enough. You cannot control what is said — only what is true.
search_keywords: talking about me behind my back
making me look like the bad guy

behaviors.plain_language_label: Cutting you off from people who support you
behaviors.plain_language_description: The person systematically reduces your access to friends, family, or support systems — through direct discouragement, manufactured conflict with those people, relocation, or making your outside relationships feel unsafe or costly.
behaviors.example: Over time, your friendships have quietly faded. When you try to maintain them, the person expresses hurt, jealousy, or criticism of the people you are seeing. You find it easier to disengage from your support network than to manage the friction.
behaviors.internal_reframe: Your support network is not a threat to a healthy relationship. Whoever works to reduce it is managing their own access to you.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: Systematically reducing another person's access to friends, family, and support systems is formally recognized in abuse research and by the CDC as a primary form of intimate partner violence. Social isolation removes the people who might offer reality-testing, alternative perspectives, or a way out. It also increases dependency on the controlling person as the primary emotional resource — which makes leaving feel structurally impossible.
depth_layer.function: Total dependency is created by eliminating alternative support. The cost of leaving rises as the support network shrinks — there is nowhere to go and no one left who knows the full story.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from natural relationship changes over time, introversion, or relocation-related social disruption. The pattern to look for is directional consistency — changes that reliably benefit the controlling person's access while reducing yours.

behaviors.response_script: Your relationships outside this one are not the problem. Maintaining them — even quietly, even imperfectly — is an act of self-preservation. You are allowed to keep your people.
search_keywords: manufacturing conflict with my support system
making it hard to see people I love

behaviors.plain_language_label: Pulling you back in after a conflict or separation
behaviors.plain_language_description: After a conflict, a separation, or after you have started pulling away, the person comes back with overwhelming affection, promises of change, or expressions of need — in ways designed to pull you back in before anything has actually changed.
behaviors.example: You create distance or end things. They begin reaching out with declarations of love, promises to be different, or reminders of the good times — drawing you back emotionally before any real change has happened.
behaviors.internal_reframe: How hard they are coming back is not evidence that things will be different. It is evidence of how much your distance threatens them. You are allowed to hold your decision.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: Pulling someone back into a relationship after they have tried to leave — through overwhelming affection, promises of change, or expressions of need — is documented in clinical literature on narcissistic abuse and the cycle of violence. Research on traumatic bonding found that the bonds formed under patterns of alternating harm and warmth are among the most persistent — often remaining strong long after a relationship ends.
depth_layer.function: The re-approach restores supply and control by reversing the exit. The love bombing quality temporarily reactivates the early feeling of the relationship, giving a biochemical reminder of why you stayed.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from genuine relational repair with demonstrable behavioral change, healthy re-engagement after reflection, or mutual reconciliation. The pattern to look for is the absence of real accountability — intensity without any genuine reckoning with the behaviors that caused the rupture.

behaviors.response_script: Intensity is not accountability. Before responding to re-engagement, ask: has anything actually changed, or is this the beginning of the same cycle? You are allowed to take time before deciding.
search_keywords: trying to pull me back in
reconciliation that feels like the beginning

behaviors.plain_language_label: Embarrassing or degrading you in front of others
behaviors.plain_language_description: The person criticizes, mocks, corrects, or exposes your vulnerabilities in front of others — in ways that create shame and are difficult to address in the moment without making the situation worse.
behaviors.example: They make a disparaging comment about your competence, appearance, or choices in a group setting. The public context makes it difficult to respond — defending yourself can seem like overreacting, but silence can feel like absorbing the attack.
behaviors.internal_reframe: What happened in that moment was real. The audience does not determine whether it was acceptable. It was not.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: Being criticized, mocked, or exposed in front of others is documented in shame research as one of the most potent shame-induction experiences — more destabilizing than private criticism because it recruits the social threat system alongside the personal one. Neuroscience research has established that social pain activates the same brain regions as physical pain. In narcissistic presentations, public humiliation serves a double function: it extracts attention from the audience while delivering punishment to the target.
depth_layer.function: Shame delivered publicly is amplified by the audience and leaves the target with no good options for response. Defending yourself is visible. Staying silent feels like absorbing the attack.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from public disagreement, legitimate accountability in group settings, or cultural norms around direct communication. The pattern to look for is the power differential — one person is diminished in front of people who witness it but do not challenge it.

behaviors.response_script: In the moment, a brief steady response or silence is enough. 'That landed as hurtful' said calmly is sufficient. Afterward, you are allowed to name what happened clearly to yourself and to trusted others. You do not have to minimize it because it happened in public.
search_keywords: humiliating me in front of others
criticizing me in front of people
called out in front of a group
made to feel small in public

behaviors.plain_language_label: Making you feel responsible for their pain
behaviors.plain_language_description: The person consistently positions their distress, disappointment, or suffering as caused by your actions — using your sense of responsibility and care as leverage to modify your behavior or prevent you from asserting limits.
behaviors.example: When you decline a request or assert a boundary, they respond with expressions of deep hurt, physical symptoms, or comparisons to how much they have sacrificed — until you reverse your decision to relieve their apparent pain.
behaviors.internal_reframe: Caring about someone's pain is not the same as being responsible for it. You are allowed to hold a limit without being the cause of their suffering.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: Using another person's empathy and care against them is documented in clinical literature as emotional blackmail — a framework identifying Fear, Obligation, and Guilt as the primary mechanisms through which manipulative people maintain control. In schema therapy, this tactic activates the Self-Sacrifice schema — the deep belief that you must meet others' needs at the cost of your own — and the Subjugation schema. Your own care becomes the mechanism of your compliance.
depth_layer.function: Prosocial emotions — empathy, responsibility, care — are turned against autonomous action. Guilt is more effective as a control tool than anger because the compliance comes from the victim's own values rather than from fear.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from genuine expressions of hurt that invite dialogue, honest communication about the impact of your actions, or appropriate disappointment. The pattern to look for is the function — pain that consistently and predictably results in you reversing your own decisions.

behaviors.response_script: You are allowed to care about their pain and maintain your decision simultaneously. 'I can see this is hard for you and I'm still holding this boundary' is a complete response. Guilt is not evidence of wrongdoing.
search_keywords: making me feel responsible for how they feel
can't say no without them falling apart

behaviors.plain_language_label: Suddenly withdrawing affection and treating you as worthless
behaviors.plain_language_description: After a period of idealization, the person shifts to treating you with contempt, dismissiveness, or as if you never mattered — sometimes abruptly, sometimes gradually — leaving you confused about what changed and what you did wrong.
behaviors.example: The person who treated you as exceptional begins responding to you with irritation, indifference, or contempt. You cannot identify what changed. You find yourself working harder to recover the version of them who valued you.
behaviors.internal_reframe: What changed was not your worth. What changed was your position in a dynamic that was never actually about you.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: The shift from idealization to contempt and dismissal is the most widely documented pattern in narcissistic abuse clinical literature. Psychologically, it reflects splitting — the inability to hold an integrated view of another person as both good and bad simultaneously. When someone stops providing perfect admiration, they shift from all-good to all-bad in the other person's experience. The victim's confusion is clinically predictable: the shift in treatment is not proportionate to anything they actually did.
depth_layer.function: The devaluation phase extracts supply through the victim's attempts to recover the positive dynamic. It also pre-empts the narcissistic injury of being left — by controlling the discard, the person maintains the dominant position.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from natural relationship changes, legitimate grievances that shift relational quality, or depression-related withdrawal. The pattern to look for is the disproportionality — treatment that shifts dramatically without a corresponding change in your behavior.

behaviors.response_script: You are not required to work harder to recover their approval. The shift in treatment tells you something important about the nature of the relationship. You are allowed to use that information.
search_keywords: went from loving me to ignoring me
feels like I don't matter anymore
working harder to get back to how it was

behaviors.plain_language_label: Being identified as the problem in the family or group
behaviors.plain_language_description: In a family or group context, you are consistently identified as the source of problems, conflict, or dysfunction — while other members' contributions to the system are minimized or ignored.
behaviors.example: Family conflicts consistently loop back to your behavior, reactions, or choices as the causal factor. Your responses to dysfunction are treated as the dysfunction. The role feels permanent and structural rather than situational.
behaviors.internal_reframe: The scapegoat is assigned a role, not given an accurate diagnosis. The role says more about the system's need than about your actual character.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: Being consistently identified as the source of problems in a family or group system is documented in family systems theory as scapegoating — a process in which the system's anxiety and dysfunction are concentrated in one member, which allows others to avoid examining their own contributions. In narcissistic family systems, a parent with NPD traits often requires a designated failure in the family to maintain their own self-narrative.
depth_layer.function: The scapegoat absorbs the family's difficulties, protecting the system's homeostasis and the narcissistic parent's self-image. The scapegoat is often the most perceptive, most honest member of the family — the one most likely to name what is actually happening.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from individual accountability for specific behaviors, constructive family feedback, or temporary role assignment during crisis. The pattern to look for is permanence — the role does not shift regardless of behavior — and the function it serves in the larger family dynamic.

behaviors.response_script: The role was assigned. You are not required to accept it as accurate. Naming the dynamic — even just to yourself — is the beginning of stepping outside it.
search_keywords: my reactions are always the issue
feel like I'm carrying the family's dysfunction
nothing is ever anyone else's fault in this family

behaviors.plain_language_label: Using faith or morality to control or shame you
behaviors.plain_language_description: The person invokes religious, spiritual, or moral frameworks to justify their control, silence your concerns, or induce shame — positioning God, morality, or spiritual authority as validation of their position and condemnation of yours.
behaviors.example: When you assert a boundary or name harmful behavior, they respond with scripture, spiritual language, or moral framing that positions your response as sinful, faithless, or spiritually immature.
behaviors.internal_reframe: Spiritual language can be weaponized. Faith that is healthy does not require your silence or your self-erasure.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: Using religious, spiritual, or moral frameworks to justify control, silence concerns, or produce shame is documented in clinical literature on religious abuse and spiritual coercion. It is distinct from scrupulosity — a form of anxiety — in that the person using spiritual weaponization is not personally distressed by the moral framework.
