depth_layer.differential: This is different from genuine perspective differences about severity, honest context-offering, or authentic processing of a difficult moment. The pattern to look for is the consistent direction — reframing that always reduces the significance of the controlling person's behavior and increases the significance of your reaction to it.

behaviors.response_script: You do not need them to agree that it was harmful for it to have been harmful. 'I experienced that as hurtful and that is what I am naming' is a complete statement. Their reframe does not require your acceptance.
search_keywords: you're too sensitive about what happened
my reaction is the problem not what they did

behaviors.plain_language_label: Changing the rules so you can never quite succeed or resolve anything
behaviors.plain_language_description: The expectations, standards, or terms of resolution keep changing — so that what was acceptable yesterday is now insufficient, and meeting one requirement leads immediately to a new one. You can never quite get it right, and the definition of right keeps moving.
behaviors.example: You addressed the concern they raised. Now the concern has changed — or the same concern is raised again as if your response did not happen. You met the standard they set. The standard has shifted. You achieve what was asked. Something else was actually what they meant.
behaviors.internal_reframe: Goalposts that move are not yours to chase. The problem is not that you are falling short — it is that the target is being managed to ensure you always fall short.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: Goalpost shifting is documented in clinical literature on narcissistic abuse as a tactic that ensures the victim remains in a permanent state of inadequacy-based striving — there is always something more to do, something that was not quite right, something they missed. It is related to chronic fault-finding but operates specifically in the domain of agreements, standards, and conflict resolution. Research on coercive control documents the manipulation of criteria for acceptable behavior as a primary mechanism for maintaining control — the rules exist not to be met but to be a source of perpetual failure. In cognitive terms, this activates learned helplessness over time: the victim eventually stops trying because no effort produces resolution.
depth_layer.function: Maintains the victim in a permanent striving posture, which serves as ongoing supply and ongoing control. Also prevents genuine conflict resolution — every conversation ends without resolution, which means the controlling person never has to change and the victim never gets closure.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from genuine complexity in resolving conflict, evolving needs in relationships, or honestly discovering new aspects of a concern. The pattern to look for is the retroactive quality — standards that shift after you have met them, and the consistent direction — shifts that always keep you one step short.

behaviors.response_script: When the terms keep changing, the problem is the terms — not your ability to meet them. You are allowed to name what you observe: 'I addressed what you raised and now the concern has shifted. I'm not going to keep chasing a moving target.' Then stop.
search_keywords: met what they asked and now there's something else
nothing I do is ever enough

behaviors.plain_language_label: Conversations that go in circles and never reach resolution
behaviors.plain_language_description: When you raise a concern or try to resolve something, the conversation spirals into confusion — the original point gets buried under accusations, deflections, unrelated grievances, and circular logic. You end the conversation more disoriented than when you started, and nothing was resolved.
behaviors.example: You bring up one thing. They bring up three others. You address those and they return to the first — framed differently. The conversation is exhausting and endless. You leave unclear about what actually happened, feeling like you might be the problem, and nothing has changed.
behaviors.internal_reframe: A conversation that consistently ends in your confusion and their escape from accountability is not a failed communication attempt. It is the communication working exactly as intended.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: Circular argument and conversational derailment are documented in clinical literature on narcissistic communication as deliberate accountability-avoidance tactics — the goal is not resolution but exhaustion and disorientation. The term 'word salad' is used in survivor communities to describe this pattern, though clinically that term refers to disorganized speech in psychosis. The manipulative use of circular conversation was documented in practitioner literature by George Simon and in trauma-informed coaching contexts as a pattern that combines blame shifting, projection, deflection, and reframing to ensure the original concern is never addressed. Research on emotional exhaustion in abusive relationships identifies chronic conversational chaos as a primary contributor to the victim's depletion and compliance.
depth_layer.function: Prevents accountability by making the conversation so exhausting and disorienting that the victim either gives up or becomes so dysregulated that their responses can be used against them. The person doing the derailing often remains calm while the victim becomes increasingly agitated — which then becomes evidence of the victim's dysregulation.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from complex conversations that take time to resolve, emotional conversations where both people become activated, or genuine communication differences. The pattern to look for is the consistent outcome — you regularly end conversations more confused and more self-doubting than when you began, and nothing is ever actually resolved.

behaviors.response_script: You are allowed to end a conversation that is going nowhere. 'I don't think we are getting anywhere right now. I'm going to step away.' Then do it. You do not have to stay in a conversation designed to exhaust and disorient you. Disengaging is not losing — it is self-preservation.

behaviors.plain_language_label: Apologies that are not actually apologies
behaviors.plain_language_description: The person offers something that has the surface structure of an apology but contains a deflection, a condition, or a reframe that transfers responsibility back to you. It sounds like accountability but functions as its opposite.
behaviors.example: 'I'm sorry you feel that way.' 'I'm sorry, but you have to understand that you also...' 'I apologize if that came across wrong.' 'I'm sorry I hurt you, but you really did provoke this.' The apology is offered and withdrawn in the same sentence.
behaviors.internal_reframe: A real apology names what was done, takes responsibility for it, and does not contain a but. What was offered was not an apology — it was the appearance of one.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: The false or strategic apology — sometimes called a fauxpology in clinical practitioner literature — is documented as a tactic used to appear accountable while avoiding genuine accountability. It is related to DARVO in that it reverses the relational dynamic — the apology is offered in a form that implicates the victim in the harm. Shannon Thomas, a trauma therapist, documents the strategic non-apology in clinical practice as a favorite maneuver that appears conciliatory while maintaining the original power dynamic. The conditional apology — 'I'm sorry if...' or 'I'm sorry but...' — is clinically distinguishable from a genuine apology by the presence of conditions, qualifications, or reframing that shift responsibility back to the victim.
depth_layer.function: Creates the appearance of accountability and relational repair without the substance — which allows the behavior to continue while the victim's legitimate response to it is neutralized. The fauxpology also produces a supply of gratitude and renewed hope that the real apology activates.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from imperfect apologies offered genuinely, apologies that include context without deflecting responsibility, or apologies followed by genuine behavioral change. The pattern to look for is the structural tell — the but, the if, the you also — and the absence of any actual behavioral change following the apology.

behaviors.response_script: You are allowed to notice that an apology did not contain accountability. You do not have to accept it as sufficient. 'I hear you saying sorry. What I needed was for you to name what you did and take responsibility for it — not explain it.' That is a complete and fair response.
search_keywords: apology that didn't feel like one

behaviors.plain_language_label: Becoming whoever you need them to be to create a fast, intense bond
behaviors.plain_language_description: Early in the relationship the person absorbs and reflects back your values, interests, tastes, and personality — creating an overwhelming sense that they truly understand you and are perfectly matched to you. The connection feels uniquely deep and fast. Later, as the mirroring fades, the person who seemed so familiar becomes unrecognizable.
behaviors.example: Within weeks they seem to share all your values, love the same things, want the same life. It feels like you have finally been truly seen. Then, gradually, their preferences shift — or you realize they never actually held those views. The person you fell for does not quite exist.
behaviors.internal_reframe: The connection you felt was real — because your values and desires are real. They were reflected back at you accurately. What was not real was the other person's genuine share of them. Your longing for that kind of connection is valid.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: Identity mirroring as a manipulation tactic in narcissistic relationships is documented in love bombing research and narcissistic abuse clinical literature. Research on love bombing identifies mirroring as a core early-relationship tactic — the person absorbs information about the target's identity during the idealization phase and uses it to construct a falsified identity the target can identify with. In object relations theory, this reflects the false self in action — a persona constructed for relational gain rather than authentic expression. In attachment research, rapid mirroring exploits the attachment system's responsiveness to felt understanding — we bond most strongly with people who seem to truly know us.
depth_layer.function: Creates rapid, intense attachment by exploiting the universal human need to be deeply known and understood. Once attachment is secured, the mirroring is no longer necessary and fades — leaving the victim confused about who they actually bonded with.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from genuine compatibility, natural resonance between people, or the normal early-relationship experience of discovery and alignment. The pattern to look for is the speed and totality — alignment that is too complete too fast — and the subsequent fading of those shared qualities as the relationship progresses.

behaviors.response_script: The connection you felt was based on something real about you. That does not disappear because the other person was not who they appeared to be. You are allowed to grieve the relationship and trust that what you want in a connection is worth wanting — it was real. They were not.
search_keywords: seemed to be exactly like me
shared all my values early on
lost who they were after we got close
felt like they knew me completely
the person I fell for doesn't exist anymore

behaviors.plain_language_label: Being left out of plans, conversations, or decisions as a form of punishment or control
behaviors.plain_language_description: The person leaves you out of social plans, family decisions, group conversations, or information — not by accident but as a deliberate signal of your reduced status or as covert punishment. The exclusion communicates something without saying it directly.
behaviors.example: Plans are made in your presence as if you are not there. Family decisions are discussed and resolved before you are informed. You find out about events after they have happened. The exclusion is consistent enough to feel intentional but deniable enough to be difficult to name.
behaviors.internal_reframe: Exclusion that happens consistently enough to feel like a pattern is a pattern. You are not imagining it, and it is not coincidence. You are allowed to name what you are experiencing even if they deny the intent.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: Deliberate social exclusion is documented in ostracism research as one of the most psychologically painful interpersonal experiences — neuroscience research has established that social exclusion activates the same brain regions as physical pain. In narcissistic presentations, exclusion functions as both a supply mechanism — the person with NPD traits maintains a position of centrality by controlling who is included — and as a covert punishment mechanism that is difficult to challenge because of its deniability. In family systems research, exclusion of specific members is documented as a scapegoating mechanism and a form of relational aggression.
depth_layer.function: Communicates diminished status and delivers punishment without the accountability of direct conflict. Deniability is preserved — nothing was explicitly done or said. The victim is left with the experience of exclusion and no clear target for their response.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from naturally overlapping social circles, oversight, or plans that predate the relationship. The pattern to look for is the consistency and the directionality — exclusion that reliably involves the same person, and that tends to coincide with periods of tension or after the victim has asserted themselves.

behaviors.response_script: You noticed. That noticing is real. You are allowed to name what you observed without needing to prove intent: 'I keep finding out about things after the fact and I'd like to be included.' What happens next will tell you something important.

behaviors.plain_language_label: Withholding attention and presence to signal disapproval or punish
behaviors.plain_language_description: The person withdraws their attention — their gaze, their engagement, their interest, their conversational presence — as a deliberate signal that you have done something wrong or failed to meet an expectation. The withdrawal is covert enough to deny but unmistakable enough to feel.
behaviors.example: You say or do something they disapprove of. Their attention subtly withdraws — they become physically present but relationally absent. They look past you, engage with their phone, respond minimally. The message is clear even though nothing is said. When you ask if something is wrong, they say no.
behaviors.internal_reframe: The withdrawal of attention as a signal is a form of communication — one that bypasses accountability by avoiding direct statement. You received the message accurately. You are not imagining it.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: Attention withdrawal as relational punishment is distinct from passive-aggressive withholding of warmth in that it specifically targets the attentional presence rather than the emotional register. In attachment research, withdrawal of attentional engagement is documented as one of the most primitive and powerful attachment threats — infants respond to the still-face experiment, in which a caregiver becomes expressionlessly unresponsive, with immediate distress. In narcissistic presentations, controlled withdrawal of attention is a powerful tool precisely because the person with NPD traits is often the primary source of attentional supply in the relationship — their attention has become the relational currency.
depth_layer.function: Delivers a precise punishment message while maintaining deniability. The withdrawal is calibrated to be felt without being nameable — which places the burden of interpretation on the victim and gaslights their accurate perception of what is happening.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from distraction, introversion, preoccupation, or natural variation in engagement. The pattern to look for is the correlation — attention withdrawal that reliably follows specific behaviors from the victim — and the deniability when named.

behaviors.response_script: You received the message. Whether they acknowledge sending it is a separate matter. You are allowed to respond to what you experienced rather than to what was officially communicated: 'Something feels different between us right now and I'd like to understand what's happening.'
search_keywords: giving me the cold shoulder without words
relationally checked out after I did something

behaviors.plain_language_label: Using prolonged silence as deliberate punishment
behaviors.plain_language_description: The person cuts off communication for an extended period — not because they need space to process, but as a deliberate act of punishment designed to produce anxiety, compliance, or capitulation. The silence is wielded as a weapon.
behaviors.example: After a disagreement or perceived slight, they stop speaking to you — sometimes for days, sometimes longer. They may be physically present but communicate nothing. The silence ends when you apologize, concede, or demonstrate sufficient distress.
behaviors.internal_reframe: Space needed for genuine processing looks different from silence used as punishment. One has an end point that is about the person's readiness. The other has an end point that is about your compliance. You are allowed to tell the difference.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: The extended silent treatment as a punishment tactic is documented in relationship research and clinical literature on emotional abuse as one of the most psychologically damaging forms of interpersonal aggression — precisely because it denies the victim the opportunity to resolve, respond, or understand. Research on ostracism establishes that social exclusion activates physical pain pathways. The silent treatment is distinguished from healthy need for space by its instrumental quality — it ends in response to the victim's behavior rather than the person's own readiness. In narcissistic presentations, the silent treatment is a covert aggression tool that punishes without accountability.
depth_layer.function: Produces anxiety, self-doubt, and compliance without the accountability of explicit conflict. The victim typically ends the silence by conceding — which teaches that silence produces the desired result and reinforces its use.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from needing time to process after conflict, introversion, or genuine emotional shutdown. The pattern to look for is the termination condition — silence that ends specifically in response to your compliance or apology, and that resumes if you assert yourself again.

behaviors.response_script: You are not required to end the silence through capitulation. You can name what you observe once: 'I notice we are not talking and I'd like to resolve what happened between us when you are ready.' Then stop pursuing. The silence says something about who holds the power in this dynamic — and you are allowed to decide how much power you give it.
search_keywords: not speaking to me for days
won't talk to me until I apologize
silence that ends when I comply

behaviors.plain_language_label: Performing helplessness to avoid responsibility and shift burden onto you
behaviors.plain_language_description: The person consistently claims inability or performs tasks so poorly that you end up doing them — not because of a genuine skill deficit but as a strategic redistribution of labor, responsibility, and cognitive load onto you. The incompetence is selective: it never applies in domains that serve their interests.
behaviors.example: They cannot seem to remember appointments, manage household tasks, or handle logistical responsibilities — but demonstrate clear competence in other areas. When you point this out, they become defensive or wounded. You find yourself managing everything while they contribute nothing to the shared burden.
behaviors.internal_reframe: Competence that is selective is not incompetence. Someone who cannot remember to schedule a doctor's appointment but can research and purchase a new piece of equipment for their hobby is demonstrating a choice, not a limitation.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: Weaponized incompetence — also called strategic incompetence — is documented in clinical practitioner literature as a covert manipulation tactic in which deliberate underperformance or claimed inability permanently redistributes labor onto another person without open negotiation. Research on over-functioning and under-functioning dynamics in couples therapy documents the self-reinforcing quality of this pattern: the more one person over-functions, the more the other under-functions, until the imbalance becomes the established relational structure. In narcissistic presentations, weaponized incompetence reflects the entitlement dimension — the expectation that labor and responsibility will be managed by others — and the covert aggression dimension — the hostility is expressed through burden-transfer rather than direct confrontation.
depth_layer.function: Permanently redistributes physical, logistical, cognitive, and emotional labor without the accountability of explicit negotiation. Also produces in the over-functioning person a sense of indispensability that can feel like purpose while actually constituting exploitation.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from genuine skill deficits, neurodevelopmental differences that affect executive function, or learning curves. The pattern to look for is the selectivity — incompetence that is domain-specific, always falling in areas that would require effort or inconvenience, and never appearing in areas of personal interest or benefit.

behaviors.response_script: You are allowed to stop compensating for competence that is selectively absent. 'I'm not going to manage this one — I trust you can figure it out' is a complete response. You do not have to rescue someone from responsibilities that are theirs to carry.
search_keywords: always helpless when it benefits them
can't do anything but can do what they want
claims they can't but they can when motivated
burden shifted onto me through helplessness

behaviors.plain_language_label: Twisting your words to mean something you did not intend
behaviors.plain_language_description: The person consistently interprets your statements, requests, or actions in the most negative or extreme light — then responds to their interpretation rather than to what you actually said. The misinterpretation positions you as the aggressor, the critic, or the unreasonable one.
behaviors.example: You make a simple request. They respond as if you accused them of something. You offer feedback. They hear attack. You ask a question. They hear interrogation. By the time you clarify what you meant, the original conversation has been replaced by one about how you said it.
behaviors.internal_reframe: The interpretation being offered is not the only available one. You know what you meant. You are not required to accept their version of your intention as more accurate than your own.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: Deliberate misinterpretation is documented in clinical literature on narcissistic abuse as a covert aggression tactic that converts neutral or positive communication into evidence of hostility — which then justifies a defensive or retaliatory response. It is documented in practitioner literature on narcissistic communication patterns as a method of avoiding accountability by responding to an invented grievance rather than the actual one. In cognitive terms, this reflects a hostile attribution bias that is strategically deployed — the assumption that neutral acts are hostile, which generates a pretext for counter-attack. The gaslighting component is significant: the victim, now clarifying intent rather than addressing the original issue, has been effectively silenced.
depth_layer.function: Converts any communication into potential evidence of the victim's hostility or inadequacy, which serves two purposes: it prevents the original concern from being addressed, and it positions the person with NPD traits as the wronged party.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from genuine misunderstanding, cultural differences in communication, or anxious interpretation that reflects the person's own history. The pattern to look for is the consistent directionality — misinterpretations that always favor the most negative reading of your intent — and the strategic effect — original concerns that are never addressed.

behaviors.response_script: You are allowed to correct the record without becoming defensive: 'That is not what I meant. What I said was...' Once. If they persist with the misinterpretation, you can disengage: 'I'm not going to argue about what I intended. I know what I meant.'
search_keywords: responding to what they thought I meant
accusing me of what I didn't intend
can't say anything without it being taken wrong

behaviors.plain_language_label: Treating you as an extension of themselves with no right to separate thoughts or feelings
behaviors.plain_language_description: The person operates as though your thoughts, feelings, preferences, and choices are naturally and rightfully aligned with theirs — and responds with confusion, hurt, or anger when you demonstrate a separate inner life. Having a different opinion, feeling, or need is experienced by them as a betrayal or rejection.
behaviors.example: You express a different opinion or feeling. They respond as if you have attacked them. They speak for you in group settings. They assume your feelings without asking. When you correct them, they are wounded that you do not feel what they assumed. Your separateness is received as hostility.
behaviors.internal_reframe: Having a separate inner life is not a betrayal of the relationship. Your thoughts, feelings, and preferences belong to you. A relationship that cannot tolerate your separateness is asking you to disappear.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: Enmeshment is a formally established concept in family systems theory — developed by Minuchin to describe family structures in which individual boundaries are diffuse and members cannot have separate psychological experiences without the system experiencing it as a threat. In narcissistic family dynamics, enmeshment reflects the person with NPD traits' inability to experience others as truly separate — a failure of what object relations theory calls differentiation. Research on narcissistic enmeshment documents how this pattern disrupts the development of a distinct autonomous self, particularly in parent-child dynamics where it often includes emotional incest and parentification.
depth_layer.function: Maintains the person with NPD traits' sense of control and centrality by eliminating the other person's separateness. A person who cannot have a different thought or feeling without it being experienced as abandonment is structurally merged with the controlling person — which makes genuine autonomy feel dangerous.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from close relationships with significant overlap in values and interests, healthy interdependence, or cultural emphases on family unity. The pattern to look for is the response to separateness — a genuine relationship can tolerate difference. Enmeshment cannot.

behaviors.response_script: Your separate inner life is not a problem to solve. You are allowed to think differently, feel differently, and want differently — and to say so. 'That is not how I feel about it' is a complete and valid statement. You do not need to merge to belong.
search_keywords: my difference is received as rejection
having my own thoughts is a problem
no space for my separate self

behaviors.plain_language_label: Being required to function as their emotional caretaker
behaviors.plain_language_description: The person consistently positions you in the role of emotional caretaker — requiring you to manage their feelings, absorb their distress, regulate their emotional state, or serve as their primary source of emotional support — in ways that reverse the appropriate relational dynamic or exceed what a healthy relationship asks.
behaviors.example: They share distress, fears, or emotional burdens that feel too large for the relationship context — and the implicit expectation is that you will hold it, solve it, or soothe it. Your own emotional needs become secondary or invisible. You feel responsible for their emotional wellbeing in a way that exhausts and destabilizes you.
behaviors.internal_reframe: You are allowed to be someone's support without being their emotional infrastructure. There is a difference between caring for someone and being responsible for their internal state. The first is love. The second is a role that belongs to a therapist — not to you.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: Emotional parentification is a formally recognized concept in clinical psychology — the process by which a child or adult is assigned the role of emotional caretaker for a parent or partner, reversing the appropriate direction of care. Research documents emotional parentification as a significant adverse childhood experience that produces long-term difficulties with self-referral, boundary setting, and distinguishing one's own needs from others'. In adult relationships, emotional parentification reflects the person with NPD traits' use of others as a regulatory resource — the victim is required to function as an external emotional regulation system. This is documented in narcissistic enmeshment literature as part of the broader pattern of treating others as extensions of self rather than separate persons.
depth_layer.function: Offloads emotional regulation onto the victim, freeing the person with NPD traits from the developmental work of self-regulation. Also maintains control — a person who is primarily focused on managing another's emotional state has very little energy left for their own needs or for noticing what is happening to them.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from mutual emotional support in healthy relationships, caring for a partner during a genuine crisis, or the natural give-and-take of emotional attunement. The pattern to look for is the structural quality — your emotional needs are chronically secondary, the burden is predominantly unidirectional, and you feel responsible for their internal state rather than simply caring about it.

behaviors.response_script: You are allowed to care deeply about someone without being responsible for their emotional state. 'I hear that you are struggling and I'm not able to be the person who holds all of this for you. I think you need more support than I can give' is a compassionate and complete response.
search_keywords: my needs are invisible compared to theirs
can't have my own feelings because theirs take up all the space
emotional labor all falls on me

behaviors.plain_language_label: Constant preoccupation with fantasies of unlimited success, power, or brilliance
behaviors.plain_language_description: The person spends significant relational time immersed in elaborate fantasies about their future greatness, exceptional achievements, or unique destiny — and expects others to participate in, validate, or orbit around these fantasies. Conversations consistently return to what they will achieve, deserve, or become.
behaviors.example: They describe in detail the success they are about to have, the recognition that is coming, the level they are about to reach — as if it is already decided. When you do not match their certainty or enthusiasm, or when reality contradicts the fantasy, they become frustrated or dismissive.
behaviors.internal_reframe: The fantasy is real to them and their need for you to validate it is real. Your failure to match their certainty is not a failure on your part — it is simply a different relationship with reality.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: Preoccupation with fantasies of unlimited success, power, brilliance, beauty, or ideal love is formally listed in DSM-5-TR as the second criterion for NPD. Network analysis research identifies this criterion as directly connected to the need for admiration and the belief in being special — the three form a self-reinforcing cluster. In self psychology (Kohut), grandiose fantasy is understood as a developmental arrest — the person never internalized a realistic sense of their own worth and remains fixated in the grandiose self-image of early development. The fantasy serves as a self-regulation mechanism: inhabiting the imagined future self compensates for the fragility of the present self.
depth_layer.function: Grandiose fantasy maintains self-cohesion and regulates fragile self-esteem. When reality does not cooperate with the fantasy, the dissonance is resolved by dismissing reality rather than revising the fantasy. This makes the person with NPD traits very difficult to reality-test with.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from healthy ambition, genuine goal-orientation, or positive visualization. The pattern to look for is the interpersonal demand — others are expected to affirm the fantasy rather than simply support the person — and the disconnection from proportionate effort or realistic assessment.

behaviors.response_script: You are not required to sustain someone else's fantasy by performing enthusiasm you do not feel. A warm, neutral response is complete: 'I hope it unfolds that way.' You do not have to match their certainty or become the audience for a narrative that requires your constant participation.
search_keywords: expecting success that isn't happening yet
expecting me to share their certainty
living in a fantasy about who they'll become

behaviors.plain_language_label: Taking advantage of people without guilt or recognition
behaviors.plain_language_description: The person routinely takes advantage of others to meet their own needs — without guilt, without recognition of the impact, and often without apparent awareness that what they are doing requires any justification. The exploitation is not experienced as taking — it is experienced as receiving what is owed.
behaviors.example: They use your connections, your resources, your time, or your goodwill in ways that benefit them significantly while offering little in return — and seem genuinely puzzled if you name the imbalance. From their perspective, helping them is simply what people do.
behaviors.internal_reframe: The absence of guilt on their part is not evidence that what happened was acceptable. It is evidence that they do not experience others as having equal standing whose resources deserve equal consideration.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: Interpersonal exploitativeness is formally listed in DSM-5-TR as Criterion 6 for NPD: taking advantage of others to achieve one's own ends. Network analysis research identifies exploitativeness and entitlement as among the most central features connecting the self and interpersonal dimensions of NPD. In object relations theory, exploitativeness reflects the inability to sustain whole-object relations — others are experienced as resources available for use rather than as persons with independent needs and standing. Research on narcissism and entitlement consistently documents that entitlement correlates with exploitativeness — the belief that one deserves what one wants naturally leads to taking it.
depth_layer.function: Maintains the narcissistic supply of resources, status, and labor without the relational cost of genuine reciprocity. Because exploitation is not experienced as taking — but as receiving what is owed — there is no internal conflict to resolve.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from accepting help naturally, relying on others during crisis, or cultural norms around reciprocity. The pattern to look for is the consistency and the absence of recognized imbalance — not a single incident of taking, but a structural pattern in which others consistently give and the person consistently receives without apparent awareness that this is unequal.

behaviors.response_script: You are allowed to notice the pattern of who gives and who receives in this relationship. You are allowed to name it: 'I've noticed I tend to give a lot in this dynamic and I'd like it to be more balanced.' What happens next will tell you something important about whether change is possible.
search_keywords: no sense that what they take is unequal

behaviors.plain_language_label: Feeling secretly superior through suffering or being misunderstood
behaviors.plain_language_description: In the covert or vulnerable presentation, grandiosity does not always look like boasting. Instead it presents as a pervasive sense of being uniquely wronged, uniquely misunderstood, or uniquely sensitive — a specialness expressed through suffering rather than achievement. The person is not like other people because they feel more deeply, are treated more unfairly, or are surrounded by people who cannot understand them.
behaviors.example: They describe their suffering in ways that position it as exceptional — no one has been through what they have, no one understands the depth of what they feel, no one appreciates how much they have endured. The pain is real, but the framing carries an implicit claim to unique status.
behaviors.internal_reframe: Their suffering may be genuinely real. The framing of it as uniquely exceptional — and the implicit demand that you recognize it as such — is where grandiosity enters. You can hold compassion for the pain without accepting the hierarchy it implies.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: Covert or vulnerable grandiosity — the expression of superiority through suffering or victimhood rather than achievement — is documented in clinical literature on vulnerable narcissism and in the research distinguishing grandiose and covert narcissistic presentations. The American Journal of Psychiatry documents the vulnerable subtype as characterized by covert grandiosity masked by apparent insecurity — the person feels and believes themselves to be exceptional but expresses this through sensitivity, specialness of suffering, or being fundamentally misunderstood rather than through explicit boasting. Network analysis research identifies grandiosity believing as common to both overt and covert presentations — the content differs but the underlying structure is the same.
depth_layer.function: Maintains the grandiose self-narrative through a socially acceptable channel. Suffering and victimhood generate sympathy and care rather than the skepticism that overt boasting might produce — which makes this a particularly effective supply mechanism.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from genuine processing of real hardship, legitimate expression of pain, or honest vulnerability. The pattern to look for is the comparative framing — suffering that is consistently positioned as uniquely exceptional, and the implicit expectation that you recognize and validate that exceptionalism.

behaviors.response_script: You can hold genuine compassion for someone's pain while noticing that the framing asks you to affirm their exceptional status. Those are two separate things. 'That sounds really hard' acknowledges the pain. You do not also have to confirm that no one has ever suffered as they have.
search_keywords: suffering that positions them as uniquely wronged
no one understands them like I do
misunderstood in a way that makes them special
victimhood with a sense of superiority

behaviors.plain_language_label: Believing rules, consequences, and social norms do not apply to them
behaviors.plain_language_description: The person consistently operates as though the standards, rules, and consequences that apply to everyone else simply do not apply to them — not through explicit argument but through behavioral assumption. When consequences do arrive, they are experienced as fundamentally unjust rather than as natural results.
behaviors.example: They arrive late consistently without apology because their time is more valuable. They expect exceptions to policies that exist for everyone. When held to a rule that applies to all, they experience it as a personal attack or injustice. Legal or institutional consequences are bewildering to them.
behaviors.internal_reframe: Rules that apply to everyone apply to them. Their experience of consequences as unjust does not make the consequences unjust — it reflects their expectation of exemption. You are not required to share that expectation or protect them from it.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: The belief that rules and consequences do not apply to oneself is an expression of entitlement — formally described in DSM-5-TR as Criterion 5: unreasonable expectations of especially favorable treatment or automatic compliance with expectations. StatPearls clinical documentation notes that legal charges are often present in the clinical history of individuals with NPD, specifically noting that they have difficulty following rules or believing rules apply to them. In schema therapy, this reflects the Entitlement/Grandiosity schema operating at its most extreme — the belief that one is above the social contract that governs ordinary people.
depth_layer.function: Maintains the grandiose self-narrative by exempting the self from the constraints that define ordinary personhood. Being subject to the same rules as everyone else is experienced as a categorical insult to one's special status.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from legitimate rule-challenging, civil disobedience grounded in principle, or cultural differences in relationship to institutional authority. The pattern to look for is the self-specific quality — rules are not opposed on principle but experienced as inapplicable to this particular person specifically.

behaviors.response_script: You are not required to create the exception they expect. The rule applies. A calm, brief statement is enough: 'This is how it works for everyone, including us.' You do not need to justify why the rule exists or why it should apply to them.
search_keywords: thinks rules don't apply to them
acts like they're above everyone else

behaviors.plain_language_label: Contempt or dismissiveness toward people they consider beneath them
behaviors.plain_language_description: The person expresses open or thinly veiled contempt, dismissiveness, or disdain toward people they perceive as being of lower status, ability, or worth — service workers, people with less education, people from different backgrounds, or anyone who does not meet their internal hierarchy of value.
behaviors.example: They are rude or dismissive to servers, disrespectful to people in service roles, or make comments that reveal contempt for people they consider ordinary. How they treat people they perceive as beneath them tells you something important about their actual values.
behaviors.internal_reframe: How someone treats people who cannot benefit them is one of the most reliable indicators of character. What you are observing is not a bad day — it is a value system.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: Disdain toward perceived inferiors reflects the DSM-5-TR criteria of arrogant and haughty behavior (Criterion 9) combined with the belief in being special and associating only with high-status people (Criterion 3). Research on the Five-Factor Narcissism Inventory identifies disdain for others and condescending attitudes as core features of the self-centered antagonism dimension of narcissism. Kernberg's object relations formulation frames contempt for others as a primitive defense — devaluing others preemptively protects against the possibility of being devalued by them. The treatment of service workers and people in subordinate roles is well-established in clinical and popular literature as a reliable behavioral indicator of narcissistic character.
depth_layer.function: Maintains the grandiose hierarchy by keeping others in clearly inferior positions. Also functions as a supply mechanism — feeling superior to others in real time generates the same self-esteem regulation as receiving admiration.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from situational frustration, cultural differences in social interaction, or assertiveness in specific contexts. The pattern to look for is the consistency and the directionality — contempt that reliably targets people perceived as lower status, and that coexists with warmth toward people perceived as high status.

behaviors.response_script: You do not have to normalize what you are witnessing. You are allowed to feel what you feel about it and to let it inform how you understand this person. 'That is not how I treat people' is a complete internal and external position.
search_keywords: dismissive of people they see as beneath them
rude to people with less status
treats some people well and others with contempt
disrespectful to people who can't benefit them

behaviors.plain_language_label: Creating or amplifying crises to pull attention and resources toward themselves
behaviors.plain_language_description: The person consistently generates, amplifies, or dramatizes crises — health concerns, relational emergencies, professional disasters — in ways that redirect attention, resources, and care toward themselves. The crises may have a real kernel but are reliably escalated beyond proportion.
behaviors.example: A minor health symptom becomes a potential serious illness requiring your immediate concern. A manageable work situation becomes a crisis demanding your attention. Problems that could be handled quietly become emergencies that reorganize everyone around them. When the crisis resolves, another appears.
behaviors.internal_reframe: You are allowed to notice the pattern. Crises that consistently resolve when they receive enough attention, and that consistently generate enough attention to preclude your own needs, are communicating something about function rather than about genuine emergency.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: Crisis manufacture as an attention-extraction mechanism is documented in clinical literature on narcissistic abuse and covert narcissism as a form of pity solicitation taken to its most activated form — not simply leading with suffering but actively generating situations that produce emergency-level care responses. It reflects the DSM-5-TR attention-seeking trait domain identified in the Alternative Model. In attachment terms, this reflects an extreme version of hyperactivating attachment strategy — escalating distress signals to ensure proximity and response. The pattern is particularly common in the vulnerable narcissism presentation where overt demand for admiration is unavailable.
depth_layer.function: Generates immediate, intensive attention and care — the emergency-level response that ordinary requests do not produce. Also effectively reorganizes everyone else's priorities around the person with NPD traits, which serves both supply and control functions.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from genuine health concerns, real crises, or appropriate escalation of serious problems. The pattern to look for is the frequency, the resolution pattern — crises that resolve when they receive sufficient attention — and the effect on your own life, which is consistently reorganized to respond.

behaviors.response_script: You are allowed to take genuine crises seriously and to calibrate your response to the pattern you have observed. 'I can see you are worried — what has the doctor said?' moves toward concrete information rather than immediate reorganization of your life around their distress.
search_keywords: health scares that resolve when I respond
constant crises that require my attention
drama that redirects everything toward them
emergencies that disappear when they get enough response

behaviors.plain_language_label: Putting others down to elevate themselves
behaviors.plain_language_description: The person consistently diminishes, criticizes, or dismisses others — colleagues, mutual friends, public figures, even your loved ones — in ways that implicitly position themselves as superior. The derogation is not random: it targets people who represent competition for status, admiration, or attention.
behaviors.example: When someone else receives recognition, they find something to criticize about that person. When you praise someone, they offer a counterpoint that diminishes the praise. When mutual acquaintances succeed, there is always a qualification. The constant message is: they are actually better.
behaviors.internal_reframe: Someone who needs to put others down to maintain their own standing has a very fragile sense of their own worth. The derogation says nothing accurate about the people being targeted.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: Competitive derogation — reducing others to elevate the self — is documented in social psychology research on self-enhancement strategies used by narcissistic individuals. Research on narcissism and social comparison shows that narcissistic individuals are particularly prone to derogating potential competitors as a self-esteem regulation strategy. In the trifurcated model of narcissism, narcissistic rivalry — the tendency to put others down, assert dominance, and compete — is identified as distinct from narcissistic admiration-seeking but equally central to narcissistic functioning. DSM-5-TR network analysis identifies antagonism as the central domain, with competitive and denigrating behaviors as primary expressions.
depth_layer.function: Generates comparative supply — superiority that comes not from one's own achievements but from the diminishment of others'. Also functions as jealousy management: derogating someone who received recognition reduces the threat their success poses to the person's self-image.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from honest critique, genuine concern about someone's behavior, or cultural norms around directness. The pattern to look for is the trigger — derogation that reliably follows others' recognition, success, or praise — and the implicit comparative function.

behaviors.response_script: You are not required to join the derogation or validate it. A brief, neutral response is enough: 'I see it differently' or simply changing the subject. You are allowed to notice what it feels like to be in a relationship where others are consistently diminished.
search_keywords: putting others down to feel better
always a criticism when someone else does well

behaviors.plain_language_label: Turning conversations into performances or lectures where you are the audience
behaviors.plain_language_description: The person consistently converts interactions into extended monologues — about their opinions, knowledge, experiences, or expertise — without genuine interest in your perspective or response. You are present as audience rather than as participant. Attempts to contribute are absorbed briefly and redirected back to them.
behaviors.example: A question you asked about something became a twenty-minute explanation of their views. A topic you introduced became a vehicle for their extended opinion. Your contributions are acknowledged minimally and used as prompts for their next monologue. By the end of the conversation you realize you have not actually spoken.
behaviors.internal_reframe: A conversation requires two people. What you participated in was a performance that required a witness. You are allowed to notice the difference and to calibrate how much of your time and attention you give to one-sided interactions.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: The monologue and lecture pattern reflects the DSM-5-TR attention-seeking trait domain and the empathy deficit criterion — the inability to sustain genuine interest in another's perspective. Research on narcissism and conversation documents that narcissistic individuals show less interest in others' experiences, are more likely to redirect conversations toward self-relevant topics, and derive less pleasure from genuine exchange than from self-expression. In psychodynamic terms, the other person functions as a self-object — a mirror who reflects the person's self-expression back without genuine independent contribution.
depth_layer.function: Extracts attentional supply through sustained audience presence. The other person's role is to provide the presence and minimal responsiveness that makes the monologue possible — genuine engagement or contribution would disrupt the supply mechanism.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from enthusiasm, expertise-sharing, or cultural differences in conversational style. The pattern to look for is the structural consistency — your role is reliably that of audience, your contributions reliably serve as prompts rather than as valued content — and the felt experience of not having actually been in a conversation.

behaviors.response_script: You are allowed to redirect: 'I'd actually like to share something too.' You are also allowed to simply be less available for conversations that only flow one direction. Your time and attention are worth more than audience participation.
search_keywords: talking at me not with me
I can't get a word in
no interest in what I think

behaviors.plain_language_label: Dominating shared environments so everything centers on them
behaviors.plain_language_description: In shared physical or social environments — family gatherings, group events, shared living spaces — the person consistently dominates the space in ways that make them the unavoidable center of attention. This may be through volume, drama, humor, crisis, or simply occupying the most relational territory.
behaviors.example: At a family gathering they are always the loudest voice, the most dramatic story, the person everyone ends up oriented toward. In shared living spaces their needs, schedules, and presence organize everyone else's. You consistently find yourself accommodating their centrality without being explicitly asked to.
behaviors.internal_reframe: A room that consistently centers one person is not an accident — it is a pattern that others have learned to accommodate. Your accommodation of it is not a character flaw. It is a learned response to an established dynamic.

depth_layer.theoretical_grounding: Dominating shared spaces for attention is an expression of the attention-seeking trait domain identified in DSM-5-TR Section III — excessive attempts to attract and be the focus of the attention of others. It reflects the entitlement dimension as well: the shared space is experienced as appropriately organized around the person with NPD traits rather than as shared. In family systems research, the person who consistently dominates shared space functions as the emotional and attentional organizing principle of the system — everyone else's behavior is reactive to theirs. This is documented in narcissistic family systems literature as a primary mechanism of family-wide control.
depth_layer.function: Generates continuous attentional supply through environmental dominance. Also functions as control — when everyone is oriented toward one person, their preferences and reactions naturally govern group behavior.
depth_layer.differential: This is different from naturally charismatic people, cultural differences in expressiveness, or appropriate leadership in specific contexts. The pattern to look for is the structural consistency — shared spaces that reliably organize around this person regardless of context — and the accommodating behaviors of others that have developed in response.

behaviors.response_script: You are allowed to orient yourself differently in shared spaces — toward the people and conversations that interest you rather than toward the gravitational pull of their centrality. This is not a confrontation. It is simply choosing where you direct your attention.
search_keywords: center of attention at every gathering
everyone ends up oriented toward them
can't have a conversation without them in it
